section.title: Walking in the Light
section.sectionRationale: John states the letter's foundational message, God is light, and draws out what it means to walk in that light honestly. He exposes three false claims about sin and answers each with the cleansing blood of Jesus, faithful forgiveness, and the advocate who is Himself the atoning sacrifice for our sins.
section.thesis: Because God is light with no darkness at all, those who truly know Him walk in the light and deal honestly with their sin, and God faithfully forgives and cleanses them through the blood of Jesus, their advocate and the atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole world.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the message that God is light; walking in the light versus claiming fellowship while in darkness; three false claims about sin met by confession and cleansing; and Jesus our advocate and atoning sacrifice.

verses.text: And this is the message we have heard from Him and announce to you: God is {{phos|light}}, and in Him there is no {{skotia|darkness}} at all.
If we say we have fellowship with Him yet {{peripateo|walk}} in the darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth.
But if we walk in the light as He is in the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus His Son {{katharizo|cleanses}} us from all sin.
If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
If we {{homologeo|confess}} our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
If we say we have not sinned, we make Him out to be a liar, and His word is not in us.
My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you will not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an {{parakletos|advocate}} before the Father, Jesus Christ, the Righteous One.
He Himself is the {{hilasmos|atoning sacrifice}} for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world.

phos.gloss: Light. To say God is light speaks of His holiness, purity, and truth, and of His self-revelation. In Him there is no darkness at all, no shadow of evil, deceit, or sin. Light is both what God is and what He shines out so that we may see and be exposed.

skotia.gloss: Darkness. In John it stands for moral evil, ignorance of God, and the realm opposed to Him. To walk in darkness is to live in sin and falsehood while perhaps still claiming to know God. There is no darkness in God, so fellowship with Him cannot coexist with a settled life in darkness.

peripateo.gloss: To walk, and figuratively to conduct one's whole life. It pictures a settled direction and habit, not a single step. To walk in the light is to live an ongoing life of honesty and obedience before God, the natural path of those who know the God who is light.

katharizo.gloss: To cleanse, purify, make clean. The blood of Jesus cleanses from all sin, an ongoing cleansing for those who walk in the light. It carries the imagery of ritual purification now fulfilled: the sacrifice of the Son truly removes defilement, not merely covers it.

homologeo.gloss: To confess, to say the same thing as, to agree openly. To confess our sins is to agree with God about them rather than deny or excuse them. It is the honest response of one walking in the light, and God meets it with faithful, just forgiveness.

parakletos.gloss: Advocate, one called alongside to help, a defender or counsel for the defence. Jesus stands before the Father on behalf of sinners. The same word names the Spirit in John's Gospel; here the risen, righteous Christ pleads our case on the ground of His own finished sacrifice.

hilasmos.gloss: Atoning sacrifice, propitiation, the offering that turns away wrath and covers sin. Jesus is Himself the hilasmos for our sins. It means both that our guilt is expiated and that God's righteous anger against sin is satisfied at the cross, so the Advocate pleads on the basis of a real payment.

basic.context: John gives the headline message he received from Jesus: God is light, utterly pure, with no darkness in Him. So people who truly know God live honest lives in the light. John then knocks down three lies people tell about sin, that we have fellowship while living in darkness, that we have no sin, that we have not sinned, and he answers each with the blood of Jesus, God's faithful forgiveness, and Jesus our advocate who is the atoning sacrifice for sins.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage holds two truths together that some pull apart. It calls us to walk in the light and not sin, yet it insists that anyone who claims to have no sin is a liar. The resolution is confession: walking in the light means bringing sin honestly to God, who forgives and cleanses through Christ. It is written both so we will not sin and so we know what to do when we do.
basic.originalLanguages: God is light, phos, with no darkness, skotia, at all. To walk, peripateo, in the light is a settled way of life, and there the blood of Jesus cleanses, katharizo, us. When we confess, homologeo, our sins, God forgives, because we have an advocate, parakletos, Jesus Christ, who is Himself the atoning sacrifice, hilasmos, for our sins.
basic.typology: The blood that cleanses and the atoning sacrifice fulfil the Old Testament sacrificial system, especially the Day of Atonement, where blood turned away wrath and covered sin. What the animal offerings pictured, the blood of Jesus accomplishes, cleansing from all sin once for all.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son whose blood cleanses, the righteous advocate who pleads for sinners before the Father, and the atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole world. Our forgiveness rests not on our performance but on His finished work and His ongoing intercession.
basic.god: God is light, holy and true, and He is also faithful and just to forgive. His justice and His mercy meet at the cross: because Jesus is the atoning sacrifice, God can forgive sin without ignoring it. He is honest about our sin and gracious in dealing with it.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 16 sets out the Day of Atonement that this fulfils. Romans 3 says God is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. Hebrews 7 and 9 present Christ's cleansing blood and heavenly intercession.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story of a holy God making a way to dwell with sinners reaches a peak here. Because God is light, sin must be dealt with, and because Jesus is the atoning sacrifice and advocate, it truly is. Confession, not pretence, is the path of those walking with the God who is light.

context.literary: The body of the letter begins with a thesis, God is light, followed by a series of conditional statements, 'If we say', that expose false claims and set out true responses. It builds to the climax of chapter 2 verses 1 and 2, where Jesus is named advocate and atoning sacrifice.
context.historical: The false teachers seem to have downplayed sin, perhaps claiming a spiritual enlightenment that made moral failure irrelevant. John counters that God is light and that honesty about sin, not its denial, marks those who know Him.
context.canonical: The passage draws on the sacrificial theology of Leviticus and joins the New Testament witness to Christ's atonement and intercession in Romans, Hebrews, and John's Gospel, where Jesus is the light of the world and the Lamb of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish that God is light, to expose false claims that deny or excuse sin, and to assure believers that walking in the light means honest confession met by the cleansing blood, faithful forgiveness, and the advocacy and atonement of Jesus Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage both describes reality, God is light, all have sin, and prescribes response, walk in the light, confess your sins. The promise that God is faithful and just to forgive is a standing assurance for all who come to Him honestly through Christ.

debates.question: How can John say 'so that you will not sin' yet also say anyone who claims to have no sin is a liar?

views.label: Both are true: aim at holiness, be honest when you fail
views.case: John writes so that his readers will not sin, holding out real holiness as the goal, while insisting that all still sin and must confess. The two are not in tension but describe the normal Christian life: pursuing obedience, and dealing honestly with failure through Christ. This is the strongest reading and it fits the whole letter, which both calls to righteousness and provides an advocate for sinners.

views.label: Sinless perfection is attainable now
views.case: Some read 'so that you will not sin' as teaching that believers can reach a state of not sinning; but this collides directly with verses 8 and 10, which call such a claim self-deception.

views.label: Sin does not really matter for believers
views.case: Others, like the false teachers, treat sin lightly since God forgives; but John's whole point is that God is light and sin must be honestly confessed, not excused.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading holds both truths: God calls His children to genuine holiness and takes sin seriously, and He provides full forgiveness and cleansing when they sin and confess. Assurance rests on Christ the advocate and atoning sacrifice, not on a claim to be already sinless.

debates.question: What does it mean that Jesus is the atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole world?

views.label: His sacrifice is sufficient for all and effective for all who believe
views.case: The cross is of infinite worth, genuinely offered to the whole world, Jew and Gentile alike, and actually saves all who trust Him. This reading honours the universal scope of the language and the particular application to those who confess and believe. It is the mainstream orthodox reading across traditions.

views.label: It stresses that salvation is not limited to one nation or group
views.case: Some emphasise that John counters any narrowing of the gospel: the atonement reaches beyond the readers to people of every nation, not only to us.

views.label: It teaches that every person without exception is finally saved
views.case: Taken to mean universal final salvation, this clashes with the letter's insistence that whoever does not have the Son does not have life, 1 John 5:12.

debates.bearing: The strongest reading affirms both the boundless sufficiency of Christ's atoning sacrifice, truly for the whole world, and its saving effect for all who believe, while denying that all are automatically saved apart from having the Son. The Advocate pleads a real, sufficient payment for sinners.

study.originalLanguages: The message is stark: God is phos, light, and in Him there is no skotia, darkness, at all. To walk, peripateo, in the light is not a single act but a settled manner of life, and there the blood of Jesus keeps on cleansing, katharizo, from all sin. Denial of sin is self-deception; but to confess, homologeo, to say the same thing God says about our sin, brings faithful forgiveness. The ground of it is a person and a work: Jesus Christ, our parakletos, the advocate called alongside to plead for us before the Father, who is Himself the hilasmos, the atoning sacrifice that both covers our guilt and turns away wrath.

typology.type: The blood that cleanses from all sin
typology.fulfillment: The sacrificial blood of the Old Testament, especially the Day of Atonement in Leviticus 16, fulfilled in the blood of Jesus that truly purifies, Hebrews 9:14.
The mercy seat and sin offering where wrath was turned away, fulfilled in Christ who is Himself the propitiation for our sins, Romans 3:25.
The high priest who entered God's presence for the people, fulfilled in the risen Christ who ever lives to intercede, Hebrews 7:25.

study.christ: Christ stands at the centre as Son, advocate, and atoning sacrifice. His blood cleanses from all sin; as the Righteous One He pleads our case before the Father; and He is Himself the propitiation that satisfies God's justice against our sin. Because the payment is real and sufficient for the whole world, and because He ever lives to intercede, the sinner who confesses has sure forgiveness grounded not in personal merit but in Him.
study.god: God is light, the very picture of holiness, purity, and truth, with no darkness in Him at all. Yet the same God is faithful and just to forgive. His justice is not set aside but satisfied in the atoning sacrifice of His Son, so that forgiveness is both free and righteous. He refuses to indulge our denial of sin, and He delights to cleanse all who come to Him honestly through Christ.

crossReferences.note: On the Day of Atonement the people are cleansed from all their sins, the pattern fulfilled in the blood of Jesus that cleanses from all sin.
God presented Christ as a propitiation, so that He is both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.
Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him, the advocacy John describes.
Jesus is the light of the world; whoever follows Him will never walk in darkness, matching the call to walk in the light.

study.oneStory: From Eden onward the Bible asks how sinners can dwell with a holy God. Here the answer rings clear: God is light and will not wink at sin, yet He is faithful and just to forgive because Jesus is the advocate and the atoning sacrifice for our sins. The way to walk with the God who is light is not to deny our darkness but to confess it and be cleansed by the blood of His Son, the very reconciliation the whole story has been moving toward.

sermon.title: Three Spurious Claims
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 1 John 1:5-2:2, on God as light, the three false claims about sin, and the cleansing blood and advocacy of Jesus Christ. A clear, orthodox handling of confession and assurance.

quiz.q: What is the message John says he heard from Jesus and announces?

options.text: God is light, and in Him there is no darkness at all.
options.note: Verse 5 states the foundational message: God's perfect holiness and purity.

options.text: God is loving and therefore overlooks all sin.
options.note: God is love, but here the message is that He is light; sin is dealt with, not overlooked.

options.text: God is distant and cannot be known.
options.note: The letter proclaims a God who reveals Himself and offers fellowship.

options.text: God is powerful and rules over all things.
options.note: God is sovereign, but the specific message named is that God is light.

quiz.q: According to verse 9, what happens when we confess our sins?

options.text: God, being faithful and just, forgives our sins and cleanses us from all unrighteousness.
options.note: Confession is met by God's faithful and just forgiveness grounded in Christ.

options.text: God forgives only after we have made up for the wrong.
options.note: Forgiveness rests on Christ's atoning work, not our making amends.

options.text: God overlooks the sin as if it never mattered.
options.note: God does not overlook sin; He forgives it justly because of the atoning sacrifice.

options.text: God forgives, though it strains His justice to do so.
options.note: It does not strain His justice; He is faithful and just to forgive because of the cross.

quiz.q: Who does John say we have when anyone sins?

options.text: An advocate before the Father, Jesus Christ, the Righteous One.
options.note: Verse 2:1 names Jesus as our advocate who pleads for us before the Father.

options.text: A set of rules that restore us if followed carefully.
options.note: Our standing rests on the advocate, not on rule-keeping.

options.text: No help at all; we must simply try harder.
options.note: We are not left alone; we have Jesus as our advocate and atoning sacrifice.

options.text: Other believers who forgive us in God's place.
options.note: Believers can encourage confession, but the advocate before the Father is Jesus Himself.

quiz.q: What does John mean that Jesus is the atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole world?

options.text: His sacrifice is sufficient for all and effective for all who believe.
options.note: The cross is of boundless worth, truly for the world, and saves all who trust Him.

options.text: The gospel reaches beyond one nation to people of every kind.
options.note: True in part, but it also means His atoning work is fully sufficient, not merely wide in offer.

options.text: Every person is automatically saved regardless of faith.
options.note: This clashes with 1 John 5:12; life belongs to those who have the Son.

options.text: Only a select few could ever benefit from it.
options.note: John stresses the whole world, not a narrow benefit.

quiz.q: How does John hold together 'so that you will not sin' and 'if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves'?

options.text: Aim at real holiness, yet be honest and confess when you sin.
options.note: Both are true: pursue obedience, and deal honestly with failure through Christ.

options.text: Believers can reach sinless perfection in this life.
options.note: This collides with verses 8 and 10, which call such a claim self-deception.

options.text: Sin does not really matter because God forgives.
options.note: John takes sin seriously; confession, not indifference, is the path.

options.text: We should expect to sin constantly and not resist it.
options.note: John does write so that we will not sin; failure is met by grace but not embraced.

questions: God is light, with no darkness at all. How does the holiness and purity of God shape the way you come to Him with your sin?
John contrasts pretending we have no sin with confessing it honestly. Where are you tempted to excuse or hide sin rather than bring it into the light?
The blood of Jesus cleanses us from all sin. What difference does it make to know the cleansing is ongoing and complete, not something you must earn?
Jesus is your advocate before the Father. How does it change your prayers and your conscience to know that the Righteous One is pleading your case?
Jesus is the atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole world. How does the sufficiency of His sacrifice give you both assurance and boldness to share the gospel?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts omit 'But' at the start of the verse, as the BSB footnote notes; the sense of contrast with verse 6 is unchanged.
'Cleanses' translates a present tense, indicating ongoing cleansing for those who walk in the light, not a one-time event only.
'Atoning sacrifice' renders the Greek hilasmos, which the BSB footnote gives as 'propitiation'; it carries both the covering of sin and the turning away of God's wrath.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 1:5-2:2. The em dash in 2:1 ('before the Father, Jesus Christ') is rendered as a comma per Makor style. Verse 2:2 footnote 'Or the propitiation' noted.
